_meta.description: Curated, scholar-grounded surah summaries. Source attributions follow Athari/Salafi-compatible classical works (Ibn Kathir introductions, Tafsir as-Sa'di, At-Tabari) without sectarian framing. Surahs not listed here fall back to a tafsir-RAG retrieval at runtime.

1.theme: The Opening — a complete supplication asking Allah for guidance to the straight path.
1.context: Recited in every unit of every prayer. Called Umm al-Kitab (the Mother of the Book) and Al-Sab' al-Mathani (the seven oft-repeated). Encapsulates the entire message of the Quran: knowing Allah, worshipping Him alone, and asking for guidance.

2.theme: The longest surah — a comprehensive constitution covering belief, worship, law, history, and warning.
key_topics: The three groups: believers, disbelievers, hypocrites
2.context: Revealed over several years in Madinah. Establishes the believing community's identity, legal framework, and posture toward earlier scripture-holders. Ayah 255 (Al-Kursi) is the greatest verse in the Quran per Sahih Muslim 810.

3.theme: Family of 'Imran — establishing the truth of tawhid against the People of the Book.
key_topics: Story of Maryam and 'Isa (peace be upon them)
3.context: Madani, paired with Al-Baqarah in the Prophet's words as the two 'Zahrawan' (the two luminous ones). Addresses Christian theology directly and draws lessons from the setback at Uhud.

18.theme: Four trials — faith (the cave), wealth (the two gardens), knowledge (Musa and Khidr), and power (Dhul-Qarnayn).
18.context: Reciting the first or last ten verses on Friday is reported to be a protection against the trial of the Dajjal (Sahih Muslim 809). The four narratives systematically address the four worldly trials.

36.theme: The heart of the Quran — affirming prophethood, resurrection, and Allah's signs in creation.
36.context: Called Qalb al-Quran (the heart of the Quran) in some narrations. Densely affirms the three Makki themes: tawhid, prophethood, and the hereafter.

55.theme: The Most Merciful — cataloguing Allah's favors on jinn and humans with the recurring refrain.
key_topics: Paradise (two gardens, and two more below them)
55.context: Known as 'Arus al-Quran (the bride of the Quran). The refrain 'Which of the favors of your Lord will you deny?' appears 31 times — a structural call to gratitude.

67.theme: The Dominion — Allah's absolute sovereignty and the certainty of accountability.
67.context: Reciting it nightly is reported to intercede for its reciter (Sunan Abu Dawud 1400, hasan). Anchors a Makki worldview: this life is a test, sovereignty belongs to Allah alone.

112.theme: Sincerity — the purest statement of Allah's oneness, free of any association.
112.context: Equivalent to a third of the Quran in reward (Sahih al-Bukhari 5013). The most concise refutation of polytheism, anthropomorphism, and incarnation theology.

113.theme: Seeking refuge in Allah from external harm — created evil, darkness, sorcery, envy.
113.context: One of the two Mu'awwidhatayn (refuge-seeking surahs). Recited daily in morning and evening adhkar and after every prayer.

114.theme: Seeking refuge in Allah from internal harm — the whispering of devils, human or jinn.
114.context: The closing surah of the Quran. Paired with Al-Falaq as the Mu'awwidhatayn: Al-Falaq protects from external harm, An-Nas from internal whispering.
